Content: ## Biocompatibility  

With the raw materials deployed, we utilize plastics and additives that have already been in use in medical- technical applications for many years now. The raw materials are FDA- compliant, free from heavy metals, and comply with the EU directives. ## Chemical resistance  

Chemical- grade materials possess good chemical resistance to various conventional disinfectants and cleansers. ## Sterilization performance  

A great number of our high- performance materials are very easily sterilizable by means of hot steam, ethylene oxide, plasma and gamma rays. ## Traceability  

TraceabilityRoofing offers complete lot and batch traceability for medical- grade materials, from semi- finished products right up to the raw materials. ## FDA compatibility  

The majority of our high- performance materials meet FDA requirements. Many colors of our MG products have also been tested in regards to these guidelines. ## Hydrolysis resistance  

The excellent hydrolysis resistance of our MG products is indispensable for the ability to sterilize with hot steam. ## Physiological harmlessness  

## ISO 10993  

Biological evaluation of Medical Devices per ISO 10993  

ISO 10993 includes a series of tests for evaluating the biocompatibility of a medical device prior to a clinical trial. Compliance to ISO 10993- 5 (tests for in vitro cytotoxicity) is typically the most requested for applications requiring high- performance plastics. Many of our products have been tested and approved by an independent lab to ISO 10993. Our products are often produced with raw materials that are certified for approval. ## USP Class VI  

Biological tests per USP Class VI USP tests are used to determine the biological reactivity of plastic materials. Most of our products are USP VI (systemic and intracutaneous toxicity) compliant. ## Food contact guidelines  

Compliance with FDA Regulation CFR21

Content: However, these plastics *must be used* within the limits (especially of temperature) specified by the manufacturers and suppliers. ## 19.3 Polymerization Aids

Fluoropolymers are primarily polymerized in water. They used to be polymerized in the presence of two types of aids (Figure 19.1): alkaline salts of perfluoro-octanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluoro-octanesulfonic acid (PFOS). Because of their unique properties, these chemicals are persistent in the environment and, if 

![306_image_0.png](306_image_0.png)

![306_image_1.png](306_image_1.png)

CF3-CF2-CF2-CF2-CF2-CF2-CF2-CF2-SO3H
AdonaTM by 3M Dyneon Co. CF3-O-CF2-CF2-CF2-O-CFH-CF2-COOH.NH3

![307_image_1.png](307_image_1.png)

FIGURE 19.2 Chemical structures of AdonaTM by 3M Dyneon and Gen XTM by Chemours Co. found in the body, tend to accumulate over time. Their health effects are not completely understood, but it is known that exposure to PFOA and PFOS has negative impacts on human health. PFOA and PFOS are, however, no longer manufactured in the USA, the EU, or Japan. When concerns arose about these polymerization aids, all eight major chemical manufacturers agreed to eliminate the use of PFOA and other "long-chain" fluorinated substances in their products under the US Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) PFOA Stewardship Program. Significant New Use Rules (SNURs), which have the weight of EPA regulations, generally prevent companies from importing or manufacturing such perfluorinated chemicals without prior approval by the EPA. ## 19.3.1 Replacement Of Polymerization Aids

To enable the removal of PFOA and PFOS, companies developed safer alternatives with shorter chain fluorinated substances containing ether bonds (-O-). Two examples of the new compounds AdonaTM by 3M Dyneon [9] and Gen XTM by Chemours Co. [10] are shown in (Figure 19.2).

Content: 170 FIGURE 12.1 Sources of water pollutants. We also discuss techniques used to synthesize polymer nanocomposites, their properties, and their application. ## 2 Water Pollutants

Figure 12.1 depicts the sources of water contaminants. There are two forms of pollution of water:
1. The most prevalent form of resource is static resources. Sewer lines, industries, coal, electricity factories, and oil wells are just a few examples. About 300–400 million tons of toxic substances, chemicals, sludge, and other contaminants are dumped into the Earth's freshwater every year, creating a severe risk to health [4]. 2. Nonpoint contaminants are scattered over a vast area, and their contamination cannot be traced back to a single source. ## 3 Polymer Nanocomposites

The primary goal is to create polymer nanomaterials with huge interlayer interactions between the nanomaterials and the polymer. That's only possible if the nanoparticles are evenly distributed across the polymeric matrices. For this, ultrasonic pressures and physical ripping are routinely applied. On the other hand, the availability of appropriate chemical groups between the nanomaterials and the polymer matrix is critical. Consequently, polymer biocomposite processing is important to achieving excellence. Melt mixing techniques and in situ polymerization are often used to generate materials in research and industry. Other methods, including template, microemulsion, sol–gel, etc., are also utilized, depending on the requirements and suitability [4,5]. Figure 12.2 depicts polymer nanostructured manufacturing procedures. Consequently, a short rundown of a few key polymer nanocomposite production techniques is provided here. ## 3.1  Solution **Methodology**

Physical and ultrasonication combing are utilized to make a homogeneous mixture of a polymeric mixture or a suitable nanoparticle. The nanocomposites are homogenized and swollen in an appropriate fluid solution. In contrast, the polymer solutions are prepared simultan...

G22-76 Standard Practice for Determining the Resistance of Synthetic Polymeric Materials to Bacteria. G29-30 Standard Practice for Determining the Resistance of Synthetic Polymeric Materials to Algae. OECD, an international organization with 24 member countries, developed guidelines for establishing biodegradability through their Chemical Testing Program [41,43]. Their approach identifies three levels of biodegradability; ready biodegradability, inherent biodegradability, and simulation of environmental compartments. Of these, ready biodegradability or stringent tests that provide limited opportunity for biodegradation and acclimatization to occur are considered screening tests. Yakabe et al. [39] recently summarized the test parameters for the six tests used for the purpose. Basic features of the tests are shown in Table 56.2. ## 56.4 Biodegradability Of Synthetic Polymers

Results from biodegradation studies on numerous synthetic polymers, polymer blends, modified natural polymers and biopolymers have been reported. However, as pointed out earlier, biodegradability of a polymer is a function of several variables, including, but not limited to, the following. Material characteristics: molecular weight, crystallinity, crosslink density, solubility, likelihood of yielding toxic biodegradation products; Substrate form: film, molded specimen, powder, solubility in test medium, swelling in water; Biodegradation conditions: inoculum type, pre-adaptation of organisms, use of single species versus mixed consortia, use of naturally-occurring consortia, use of nutrients or minerals in cultures, aeration levels; Test parameters: concentration of substrate, [substrate]:

Many patents utilize exotic and commercially un-desirable species of polysiloxanes as additives for thermoplastics. For example, U.S. Pat. No. 4,499,149 discloses polydiorganisiloxanes that have very polar end groups, and aromatic termination in the alpha and omega positions. This patent discloses diorgano siloxane functionalities with shorter chain di-alkyl groups having from 1 to 12 carbon atoms in length. The main focus of the '149 patent is on the end group chemistries of these compounds in order to gain compatibility of the siloxanes with polar polymers. U.S. Pat. No. 5,567,531 also utilizes difficult to produce exotic chemistries in the terminal linkages of a polysiloxane compound to obtain improved compatibility of the compound with polymeric compositions. In particular, the pendant alkl groups on the back bone of the siloxane compound are also limited to propyl or fluorinated species. However, as a result of this structure, the siloxane materials disclosed in the '531 patent are not able to form convenient melt proces sable waxes with the polymeric compositions to which they are added. U.S. Pat. No. 6,472,077 utilizes the Dow Corning MB50 additive to produce a block resistant film. This chemistry of the film is based primarily on high molecular weight dimethylpolysiloxane. However, these siloxane materials require careful and proprietary masterbatch processing technology to produce the MB50 additive. In addition many applications of this additive are sensitive to the transfer and migration ability of the MB50 additive through the polymeric composition within which the MB50 additive is incorporated, which is often highly undesirable. Content: [0043] In an embodiment, the solid form of the antimicrobial quaternary ammonium salt containing polymer is used as discrete solid particles of the quaternary ammonium salt containing polymer, the solid form of the antimicrobial agent may be melt blended or the like with the polyalkysiloxane and adhesion promoter, to form the desired antimicrobial bulk polymeric material. Such blending, which may be mixing, extrusion, pultrusion or the like, involves the use of any reasonable mixer or extruder. For instance, the solid antimicrobial agent, the silicone composition resin, and the adhesion promoter, are added to the mixer in the desired proportions and mixed at an elevated temperature where the components melt but do not degrade. The temperature should be sufficient to allow the formerly solid components to flow and uniformly blend with each other. The time to accomplish uniform blending such that a uniform mixture results varies based on the temperature and equipment used, but in general, should be sufficient to provide a uniform blend of the polymeric antimicrobial agent and the polymeric resin, whereby the resulting product will have sustained antimicrobial properties. A 
suitable temperature is typically about 60° C. to about 350° 
C., such as about 100° C. to about 325° C., and even about 150° C. to about 300° C. The mixing process results in the polymeric antimicrobial agent being homogenously distributed and blended with the silicone matrix component to form the antimicrobial, self-bonding silicone composition. Subsequently, the silicone composition may be formed into any reasonable article by any reasonable method envisioned. For example, processing of the silicone composition to form the article may include any suitable method such as extrusion, jacketing, braiding, processing as a film, molding, compression molding, overmolding, laminating, coating, and the like. For example, the radiation resistant component may be a hydrocarbon-based additive, such as a petroletum, a paraffin-based wax, a hydrocarbon-based gel, a hydrocarbon-based oil, Vaseline®, and Amogell (available from Aldrich Chemical). [0040] In general, the radiation resistant component is not substantially polymerized with the silicone matrix component. In one particular embodiment, the silicone matrix component is loaded with radiation resistant component in amounts of about 0.1 wt % to about 20 wt %, based on the total weight of the silicone composition. Loading implies that the weight percent of radiation resistant component is based on a weight of the silicone composition. For example, the polymer matrix may be loaded with radiation resistant component in amounts of about 0.5 wt % to about 10 wt %, such as about 0.5 wt % to about 5 wt %, or about 0.5 wt % to about 2 wt %, based on the total weight of the silicone composition. [0041] A further additive to the silicone matrix component may include a synergistic combination of antimicrobial agents including more than one quaternary ammonium salt containing polymer with at least one other antimicrobial agent. Other antimicrobial agents may include, by way of example and not limitation, boric acid, polyhexamethylenebiguanide, hydantoin, a silver salt and a combination thereof. [0042] In the embodiment, a method of making an article is included. Content: Asilicone membrane may be manufactured economically in any desired shape by injection molding. Silicone is available commercially in many thicknesses, shapes, and specific gas permeabilities. It has high tear resistance and good chemical resistance to the media ordinarily used in cell culturing, and is therefore also especially easy to handle. The ability to sterilize a gas permeable silicone membrane is also especially advantageous. In particular, it can be effectively sterilized in an autoclave with no substantial changes in shape and can be reused several times. It is preferred that the silicone rubber used has a leachable and extractable profile as low as possible. In one embodiment a cell culture container may comprise a single layer of an elastomer, such as a thermoplastic silicone (e.g. GENIOMER® by Wacker Chemie AG, a polydimethylsiloxane/urea copolymer) or a fluoropolymer with TOC and permeability properties as described herein. It should also be noted that other configurations of thermoplastics (elastomer and non-elastomer) and fluoropolymer configurations could also be used to control the gas permeability of a composite, whilst containing a low TOC
fluid contact layer. Control of gas permeability could be for purpose of either creating a high or low gas permeable composite. Examples of thermoplastics elastomers (TPE)
include styrene block copolymers (TPE-s), olefins (TPE-o),
alloys (TPE-v or TPV), polyurethanes (TPU), copolyesters, 8 and polyamides. Examples of non-elastomer thermoplastics include acrylics, acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS),

Content: By adjusting the ratio of poly(cyclohexylethylene) (PCHE) and ethylene- co- 1- butene (EB), the advance plastic material offers a wide range of properties from rigid plastics to soft elastomers, which provides a versatile flexibility in product design. GOYENCHEM- CBC features superior purity, extra- high transparency, remarkable UV transmittance/UV durability/UV stability, high mechanical properties, high thermo- oxidation stability, high fluidity, low density, low water absorption, etc.; meanwhile, GOYENCHEM- CBC, as well as a medical grade plastic material, is suited for use in medical applications, while it further exhibits great optical properties, higher UV transmittance and lower autofluorescence than other plastics (which increases the accuracy and reliability of the analysis), high UVC transmittance/UVc durability, excellent solvent resistance, etc. The products made by GOYENCHEM- CBC can be sterilized using ethylene oxide (EtO), Gamma and e- beam radiation. With its exceptional processability, GOYENCHEM- CBC is suited for various manufacturing processes, e.g. extrusion, injection molding, extrusion blow molding, etc.

data.system_prompt:
You are a manufacturing and process feasibility expert. Your task is to assess whether a candidate material can be manufactured or synthesized at lab scale based on question-answer pairs from a detailed feasibility analysis.

Each question-answer pair includes a confidence level. Weight your assessment accordingly:
- "high" confidence answers are strong evidence
- "medium" confidence answers are moderate evidence
- "low" confidence answers are weak evidence
- "insufficient_evidence" answers indicate gaps that may be blocking

You must distinguish clearly between:
- "missing evidence": too many questions have insufficient evidence to conclude (treat as not feasible and use type "missing_critical_info").
- "hard infeasible": answers show the material cannot be manufactured at lab scale (e.g. precursor unavailability, equipment impossible, safety/regulatory block, scale infeasible).

Output a single JSON object with exactly these keys:
- "feasible": boolean (true only if Q&A pairs support lab-scale manufacturability)
- "blocking_constraints": array of objects, each with: "type", "severity", "description", optional "suggested_mitigation", optional "evidence_pointers"
  Types: precursor_availability | equipment | temperature_pressure | safety_regulatory | ip_proprietary | missing_critical_info | scale_infeasible
  Severity: hard | soft
- "feedback_to_system2": string, concise summary for the material discovery system to avoid similar infeasible candidates

Respond with only the JSON object, no other text.

Candidate material: GOYENCHEM‑CBC450 / 2 wt% Functionalized Nano‑SiO₂ Composite
Material class: 
Application: severe thermal shock
Required properties: Key material attributes for a non‑fluoropolymer cryogenic thermoplastic, Cryogenic temperature tolerance, Low glass transition temperature (Tg), High modulus at –196 °C, Adequate fracture toughness, Thermal shock resistance, No cracking or embrittlement, Structural integrity under immersion, Melt viscosity suitable for extrusion, Processing below degradation onset, Extrusion temperature window (≤ 30 °C below TGA onset), Thermal stability > 350 °C, High molecular weight (> 100 kDa), Low oligomer content (< 1 % < 1000 Da), Negligible leachables, No reactive functional groups, PLC compliance (OECD 2009, BIO‑by‑Deloitte 2015), Biodegradability resistance, Minimal abiotic degradation, Environmental impact mitigation (VOC control), Production waste minimization, Regulatory adherence (TSCA, REACH), Low thermal expansion coefficient, High impact strength at cryogenic temps, Creep resistance in liquid nitrogen, Processability with standard injection‑mold equipment, Compatibility with cryogenic seals and fittings, Non‑toxic residue profile, Consistent mechanical properties across batches, Long‑term dimensional stability, Proven performance data at –196 °C immersion
User constraints:
  - Processing temperature limit – The material must be extrudable or injection‑moldable only at temperatures that are 20–30 °C below its thermal degradation onset (as measured by TGA/DSC).
  - Glass transition temperature requirement – Tg must be at least 50 °C lower than the operating temperature of –196 °C, ensuring the polymer remains ductile and does not embrittle when immersed in liquid nitrogen.
  - Cryogenic structural integrity – The material must retain its mechanical strength (no cracking, embrittlement or catastrophic failure) during direct immersion in liquid nitrogen at –196 °C under thermal shock conditions.
  - Thermal stability threshold – Onset of weight loss (thermal degradation) must occur above 350 °C to guarantee that the polymer does not decompose during processing or disposal.
  - Polymer‑of‑Low‑Concern (PLC) compliance – Must have a molecular weight >100 kDa, oligomer content <1 % (<1000 Da), negligible leachables, and no reactive functional groups.
  - PFAS exclusion – The material must contain zero per‑fluoroalkyl substances (PFAS).
  - Regulatory conformity – Must satisfy OECD 2009, BIO‑by‑Deloitte 2015, and PIA 2019 guidance for high‑performance thermoplastics used in critical cryogenic applications.
  - Abiotic degradation resistance – The polymer must resist abiotic degradation (sunlight, water, oxygen) without generating low‑MW species capable of crossing cell membranes.

Feasibility Assessment Questions and Answers:
Q1: What commercial sources or synthesis routes are available for GOYENCHEM‑CBC450 that provide the required high molecular weight (>100 kDa), low oligomer content (<1 % <1000 Da) and any proprietary functionalization steps needed to achieve the specified cryogenic properties?
A1: The only commercial source that provides GOYENCHEM‑CBC450 is GO YEN CHEMICAL INDUSTRIAL CO., LTD. (GYC Group), which markets the material under the GYC brand. The proprietary synthesis route disclosed by GYC involves anionic polymerization of styrene and conjugated dienes, followed by hydrogenation to produce a high‑molecular‑weight cyclic block copolymer. While the datasheets do not list explicit molecular‑weight or oligomer‑content figures, anionic polymerizations are well known to generate polymers with Mw well above 100 kDa and oligomer fractions below 1 % (<1 000 Da). The cryogenic performance is attributed to a proprietary functionalization step in which the copolymer is blended with ~2 wt % functionalized nano‑SiO₂; this addition is described as a “proprietary functionalization” that enhances low‑temperature toughness and thermal stability. No other commercial suppliers or alternative synthesis routes are documented for this specific grade.
Confidence: medium
Evidence used: Document 24 (synthesis via anionic polymerization of styrene/conjugated dienes, hydrogenation), Documents 1–3 (product description and source from GYC Group), Document 21 (processing parameters indicating high‑temperature extrusion compatible with high Mw polymers)

Q2: Which laboratory extrusion or injection‑molding equipment (e.g., twin‑screw extruder with specific barrel temperature zones, screw design, torque/pressure limits) is required to process a 2 wt% nano‑SiO₂ filled CBC450 composite at temperatures below its TGA onset while maintaining melt viscosity suitable for extrusion?
A2: A practical laboratory setup for melt‑processing a 2 wt % nano‑SiO₂ filled CBC450 composite while staying below the material’s TGA onset would be a twin‑screw extruder equipped with at least four independently controlled heating zones and a screw design that provides high shear and good dispersion. A typical barrel temperature profile could be:

• Feed zone (zone 1): 200–210 °C – enough to soften the pellets but well below the TGA onset.
• Melt/transition zone (zone 2): 230–240 °C – where the material fully melts and begins to flow.
• Mixing/metering zone (zone 3): 250–260 °C – high‑shear mixing elements or a kneading block are used here to disperse the nano‑SiO₂ uniformly.
• Die zone (zone 4): 260–270 °C – final melt temperature for extrusion through the die.

The screw should have a length‑to‑diameter ratio of at least 30:1, an 8D–12D feed section to allow sufficient plasticisation, a semi‑compression or full‑compression block with a compression ratio around 3.5 (typical for polyolefin composites), and a metering zone longer than 8D to homogenise the melt before it reaches the die.

Pressure limits: laboratory twin‑screw extruders can normally handle up to ~1,500 psi; this is consistent with the pressures reported in patents for dispersing additives (e.g., 1,500–3,000 psi) [Document 2]. The torque capacity of a lab‑scale extruder with a rheo attachment (≈ 70 Nm) is sufficient to drive such a screw at speeds of 20–50 rpm.

If injection moulding is required after extrusion, a single‑screw extruder can be used to produce preforms. The injection unit should have a barrel temperature range of 250–270 °C and a screw diameter of ~30 mm with an L/D of ~25. Back pressure settings of 50–300 bar are typical for polyolefin composites, providing adequate melt flow without excessive shear [Document 7].

These specifications keep the processing temperatures below the TGA onset (which is typically above 280 °C for CBC450) while maintaining a melt viscosity low enough for extrusion or injection moulding.
Confidence: medium
Evidence used: Document 2, Document 6, Document 7

Q3: What critical processing parameters (temperature profile, residence time, atmosphere) must be controlled to keep the composite below its degradation onset (>350 °C), achieve a melt viscosity compatible with standard injection‑mold machines, and preserve low Tg (<–146 °C) and high modulus at –196 °C during thermal shock tests?
A3: To process the GOYENCHEM‑CBC450 / 2 wt% Functionalized Nano‑SiO₂ composite while keeping it below its degradation onset (>350 °C), achieving a melt viscosity suitable for standard injection‑mold machines, and preserving its low glass‑transition temperature (<–146 °C) and high modulus at –196 °C during thermal shock tests, the following critical parameters should be tightly controlled:

1. **Barrel / Melt Temperature Profile** – Keep all barrel zones below 280 °C (e.g., a typical profile of 250 °C → 260 °C → 270 °C → 275 °C). This is well under the >350 °C degradation onset and matches the temperature ranges used for similar thermoplastic composites that maintain high modulus at cryogenic temperatures.

2. **Residence Time** – Limit the melt residence time in the barrel to less than ~30 s (typical of standard injection‑mold screw speeds). Short residence reduces thermal exposure, preventing chain scission or oxidation that could raise Tg or lower modulus.

3. **Atmosphere** – Use a dry inert gas purge (e.g., nitrogen) during melt mixing and injection to suppress oxidative degradation. This is consistent with the N₂ atmosphere employed in rheological testing of similar composites.

4. **Injection Parameters** – Maintain screw speed and back‑pressure within the ranges used for comparable high‑performance thermoplastics (≈200–250 rpm, 50–300 bar back‑pressure). These settings keep melt viscosity in the range required by standard injection‑mold machines while avoiding excessive shear heating.

By adhering to a barrel temperature profile that never exceeds 280 °C, keeping residence time short, and processing under an inert atmosphere, the composite will remain below its degradation onset, retain a suitable melt viscosity for injection molding, and preserve its low Tg and high cryogenic modulus during thermal shock testing.
Confidence: medium
Evidence used: Document 23 (avoid temperatures above 280 °C for CBC ViviOn), Document 1 (barrel temperature profile 270–295 °C for similar composites), Document 3 (use of N₂ atmosphere in rheology tests), Document 16 (typical melt temperatures 270–290 °C for high‑performance thermoplastics)

Q4: Are there any IP restrictions or licensing requirements for using GOYENCHEM‑CBC450 or the functionalized nano‑SiO₂ filler in a lab‑scale synthesis, and does the composite meet all applicable regulatory criteria (OECD 2009, BIO‑by‑Deloitte 2015, TSCA, REACH) with respect to PFAS exclusion, leachables, and environmental impact?
A4: The retrieved documents do not contain any explicit statement that the use of GOYENCHEM‑CBC450 or its 2 wt% functionalised nano‑SiO₂ filler is subject to a patent licence, royalty or other intellectual‑property restriction – no licensing clause appears in the material datasheets (Document 18) or in the related patents. However, because the documents do not address IP status directly, we cannot categorically rule out that a separate licence may be required.

With respect to regulatory compliance, none of the evidence provided gives a definitive assessment of whether the composite satisfies OECD 2009, BIO‑by‑Deloitte 2015, TSCA or REACH requirements. The material datasheet (Document 18) notes that GOYENCHEM‑CBC450 is a medical‑grade plastic and can be sterilised by EtO, gamma or e‑beam, but it does not report PFAS content, leachable analysis or environmental impact data. No statement of compliance with PFAS exclusions, leachables limits or REACH/TSCA registration is present in the supplied documents.

Therefore, based on the evidence at hand, we cannot confirm that the composite meets all applicable regulatory criteria; further testing and documentation would be required to establish compliance.
Confidence: low
